body: What you get and what we expect. Jason and Ryan run this on the side, unpaid, for people trying to break into customs and trade compliance. Read the resources in order, do the worksheets, bring real questions to the monthly session.
title: Start Here: How the Columbus Trade Mentorship Program Works

body: A plain checklist of what to learn first: HTS classification, valuation, country of origin, then entry filing. Skip the alphabet soup until you understand why each rule exists. Work through one topic a week.
title: Trade Compliance Roadmap for Your First Year

body: Every entry starts with the invoice. Know where to find the value, the Incoterm, the parties, and the goods description. If the description is vague, you cannot classify it. Send it back and ask.
title: Reading a Commercial Invoice Without Getting Lost

body: Work GRI 1 through 6 in order. Most goods stop at GRI 1 if you read the headings and notes carefully. We use a real auto-parts bracket to show how the section and chapter notes decide the outcome.
title: HTS Classification Walkthrough: General Rules of Interpretation

body: Kits and composite goods trip everyone up. The test is which component gives the article its essential character. Walk the textile-and-hardware set we use in session and you will see how to defend the call.
title: Essential Character and GRI 3(b): When Goods Are Made of Several Parts

body: Do not guess when a binding ruling already exists. Search CROSS, read the reasoning, and cite it in your file. If nothing fits, that is when you consider requesting your own ruling.
title: Using CBP Rulings and CROSS to Back Up a Classification

body: Brackets, fasteners, and sub-assemblies get jammed under the wrong heading constantly. Higher duty or a penalty later. This guide lists the headings we see confused most and how to tell them apart.
title: Common Misclassifications in Automotive Parts

body: Tariff shift versus regional value content, plain language. We trace one part through the bill of materials so you can see when a non-originating input still qualifies the finished good. Bring your own BOM and follow along.
title: USMCA Rule-of-Origin Walkthrough for Auto Parts

body: The form is short and people still get it wrong. Origin criterion, the responsible party, the blanket period. Get those three right and most of your problems disappear. We mark up a completed certification field by field.
title: Filling Out a USMCA Certification of Origin Correctly

body: Two methods, two formulas, one right answer for your situation. We show the math both ways on the same product so you can see why the method you pick changes whether the good qualifies.
title: Regional Value Content: Transaction Value vs Net Cost

body: The 10+2 elements, who provides each, and the deadline before lading. Late or wrong ISF is an easy penalty to avoid. Print the checklist and run every ocean shipment against it.
title: Importer Security Filing (ISF) Checklist

body: From the 7501 entry summary to the supporting docs CBP can ask for. We label every box on a real entry so you understand what you are attesting to when you file.
title: Anatomy of a Customs Entry Filing

body: If goods come in and go back out, you may be owed a refund of the duty. We cover the main drawback types, the records you must keep, and the time limits. Most importers leave this money on the table.
title: Duty Drawback Primer: Getting Money Back on Re-Exports

body: Transaction value is the starting point, then the additions: assists, royalties, packing, commissions. We work an invoice up to the value CBP expects so you are not surprised at liquidation.
title: Customs Valuation: Building the Dutiable Value

body: Base duty, then 301, then any other special program. Order matters and so does which HTS line the extra duty hangs on. We walk a China-origin part through the full stack.
title: Section 301 and Additional Tariffs: Reading the Stacking Order

body: The exam is open book and still hard because of time pressure. We list the regulation parts that show up most, how to tab your books, and how to budget minutes per question. Plan on a real study schedule.
title: Customs Broker License Exam Study Guide

body: Knowing where to look beats memorizing. We show how the HTSUS general notes and the parts of 19 CFR map to exam topics, so you flip straight to the answer instead of reading from the front.
title: How to Navigate the HTSUS and 19 CFR Under Time Pressure

body: A set of worked classification and valuation problems with the reasoning spelled out, not just the letter answer. Do them closed-book first, then check why the wrong choices are wrong.
title: Practice Questions: Classification and Valuation

body: A Columbus apparel importer was paying too much because of a sloppy goods description. Jordan worked the headings, found a defensible lower-duty classification, and filed a post-summary correction. Here is the reasoning.
title: Case Study: Reclassifying a Textile Import to Lower Duty

body: Carla traced a processed-food product through its inputs to prove origin and unlock preferential treatment. The interesting part is the one non-originating ingredient and how the tariff-shift rule still let it qualify.
title: Case Study: Qualifying an Ag Export Under USMCA

body: Devin worked a manufacturing drawback claim where the recordkeeping was the whole battle. We show what CBP rejected, what fixed it, and the refund that landed. The lesson is records, records, records.
title: Case Study: A Drawback Claim That Took Three Tries

body: Plain-English definitions for the terms that show up in every entry: HTS, MPF, HMF, FTZ, Incoterms, liquidation, protest, prior disclosure. Keep it open while you read the other guides.
title: Glossary of Trade Compliance Terms

body: Each term in one line: where risk transfers and who handles export and import formalities. Knowing the Incoterm tells you who your customer thinks is responsible, which is half the conversation.
title: Incoterms 2020: Who Pays and Who Files

body: Notes from our spring session on what happens when something goes wrong. When to file a prior disclosure, how mitigation works, and why fixing it yourself almost always beats waiting for CBP to find it.
title: Recorded Session Notes: Penalties, Mitigation, and Prior Disclosure
